nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, reef crests, lagoons, and rubble zones; commonly found in moderate-light, moderate-flow areas from the intertidal zone to moderate depths

decorPreferences: spacing from neighboring corals to limit allelopathic spread

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Tend to benefit from weekly target feeding of meaty foods such as Mysis shrimp, micro-plankton, brine shrimp, zooplankton, or commercial coral foods. Will also absorb dissolved nutrients from the water column.

avoidWith: neighboring corals placed in direct contact — Protopalythoa encrusts and may overgrow them
predatory snails such as box snails (known to prey on polyps)

summary: Protopalythoa are large-polyp encrusting zoanthids closely related to Palythoa, found across tropical reefs. Relatively hardy in reef tanks but elevated to intermediate care due to palytoxin handling risks and chemical aggression.
careNotes:
SAFETY WARNING — PALYTOXIN: Protopalythoa are closely related to Palythoa and have been documented to contain palytoxin, one of the most potent non-protein toxins known to science. Not every colony carries it in equal amounts, but the risk must be treated seriously.
• Always wear nitrile or latex gloves AND eye protection (splash goggles, not just glasses) when handling Protopalythoa, fragging, or doing any maintenance that disturbs the colony.
• NEVER heat, boil, or cook live rock, substrate, or water that may contain Protopalythoa fragments. Aerosolized palytoxin has caused documented poisonings, hospitalizations, and reported fatalities among home reefkeepers.
• Wash hands thoroughly after any handling even if gloves were worn.
• If palytoxin exposure is suspected (skin rash, eye irritation, tingling, respiratory distress, nausea), seek medical attention immediately and inform treating physicians of palytoxin exposure.

Taxonomy note: Protopalythoa is a distinct genus from Palythoa, though the two are closely related within family Sphenopidae. Many species have historically been reclassified between the two genera. In the aquarium trade, Protopalythoa and Palythoa are often sold interchangeably under the common names 'colony polyp', 'button polyp', or 'sea mat'. Protopalythoa tends to have larger, more distinct polyps that are not as fully embedded in the connecting tissue (coenenchyme) as Palythoa, though visual distinction in trade can be unreliable.

Related sibling entries: [palythoa-coral] (Palythoa sp.) and [zoanthus-coral] (Zoanthus sp.) are closely related zoanthid genera within the same family. Palythoa corals have polyps more deeply embedded in a shared mat; Zoanthus tends to be smaller-polyped. All three genera may contain palytoxin and share broadly similar care requirements.

Husbandry notes:
• Lighting: Generally thrives under moderate lighting (75–200 PAR). Reported as tolerant of a wide range from lower to higher light; new specimens should be acclimated gradually over 1–2 weeks by starting lower in the tank and moving upward to prevent bleaching.
• Flow: Moderate, indirect water movement is generally preferred. Direct high-velocity flow may cause polyps to remain closed; flow helps carry food particles and prevents detritus accumulation.
• Placement: As encrusting corals, Protopalythoa can be placed at any height in the aquarium. The colony will spread across available rock surfaces over time.
• Colony growth: Under favorable conditions, Protopalythoa tend to encrust and spread across available rock surfaces. They have been known to overgrow neighboring corals if not given adequate spacing. Plan for ongoing fragging/pruning to control spread.
• Allelopathy: Like most zoanthids, Protopalythoa can release chemical compounds into the water that may stress neighboring corals. Regular activated carbon or good protein skimming is generally advised in mixed-coral systems.
• Feeding: Although primarily photosynthetic, Protopalythoa have been documented to respond positively to weekly meaty target feeding (Mysis, micro-plankton, brine shrimp, commercial zooplankton blends). Feeding tends to accelerate growth and improve coloration.
• Tank size: Protopalythoa are commonly kept in nano and pico reef systems. The 76 L (20 gallon) minimum reflects typical nano reef practice; smaller systems are sometimes used but require more frequent water changes and careful attention to nutrient stability.
• Reproduction: Protopalythoa reproduce readily in captivity by budding. Fragging (cutting) is also widely practiced; full PPE (gloves, eye protection) is essential during any fragging procedure due to palytoxin risk.
breedingNotes: Protopalythoa reproduce readily in captivity by budding — new polyps emerge from the connecting tissue (coenenchyme) surrounding existing polyps, gradually expanding the colony footprint. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not deliberately practiced in home aquaria. Fragging (cutting) with bone snips or razor blades is the standard propagation method among hobbyists; PPE (gloves, eye protection) is essential during any fragging procedure due to palytoxin risk.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Colony Polyp, Nuclear Green (Protopalythoa sp.)
primary.notes: Primary product page for Protopalythoa sp. on LiveAquaria. Scientific name Protopalythoa sp. confirmed on product page. Provides: temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, lighting moderate-high, flow medium, care easy, temperament semi-aggressive.

additional.name: Reef Builders — Zoanthid & Palythoa Intro Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters for zoanthid/palythoa group (Protopalythoa not differentiated separately): temperature 75–78°F, salinity 1.025–1.026, kH 8–12, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm. Confirmed palytoxin safety warnings.

additional.name: Reef Chasers — Zoanthids & Palythoa Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 77–84°F, pH 8.0–8.4, kH 8–9, Ca 400–450 ppm, PAR 150–200 (moderate). Palytoxin described as extremely dangerous; boiling rock explicitly warned against. Care level beginner (averaged to intermediate with primary source's 'easy' given palytoxin handling requirement).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Palythoa (taxonomy and palytoxin)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation of family Sphenopidae, Order Zoantharia; confirmed palytoxin presence in closely related Palythoa and Protopalythoa genera. Not used for husbandry parameters.
